title:
A big day for the BWRX-300 small modular reactor! 

Tennessee Valley Authority, along with a coalition of U.S. utilities and supply chain partners including GEH, announced today that they have… | GE Vernova Hitachi Nuclear Energy | 49 comments
text:
A big day for the BWRX-300 small modular reactor! Tennessee Valley Authority, along with a coalition of U.S. utilities and supply chain partners including GEH, announced today that they have applied for $800 million in funding from the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) to accelerate deployment of small modular reactors and the BWRX-300. This impressive coalition includes Bechtel Corporation, BWX Technologies, Inc. (BWXT), Duke Energy Corporation, Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI), GEH, Indiana Michigan Power – an AEP company, Oak Ridge Associated Universities, Sargent & Lundy, Scot Forge, other utilities and advanced nuclear project developers and the State of Tennessee. “Nuclear power has a key role to play in reaching a cleaner and more secure energy future,” said Scott Strazik, CEO, GE Vernova. “Funding from this grant would play a critical role in the path forward and we look forward to working with TVA and this strong team of utility and supply chain partners to accelerate the roll-out of small modular reactors in the United States.” TVA has selected the BWRX-300 for potential deployment at the Clinch River Site and, if awarded this funding, plans to accelerate construction of the first SMR at the site by two years with commercial operation planned for 2033. There were two related announcements today. Duke Energy has entered into an agreement to invest in activities to advance the standard design and licensing of the BWRX-300. We are proud to have Duke Energy involved in our BWRX-300 project and welcome the insights and expertise they bring to the team. Also today, American Electric Power announced that it has selected the BWRX-300 for potential deployment at the Indiana Michigan Power Rockport Plant in Spencer County, Indiana. With its small footprint, the BWRX-300 is ideally suited to help AEP provide its customers with reliable, secure energy. “On the heels of the significant progress that is occurring with the deployment of the first BWRX-300 at Ontario Power Generation’s Darlington New Nuclear Project site, these announcements signify the growing confidence the industry has in our SMR technology,” said Maví Zingoni, CEO, GE Vernova's Power businesses. Read the full announcement here: https://lnkd.in/ewgbqVyP
Welcome to the fun, y’all.
Hi Andrew, I hope you’re well. I’m sincerely interested in SMRs, but also frustrated by timescales. When do you think this reactor will go live, and when will it go into mass production. Obviously approximate timescales. I’m still consulting, but it’s hard to advise on SMRs when timescales seem so up in the air. All the best from a cold and windy Cumbria, Marcus
Exciting times for the nuclear energy sector! The growing momentum around the #BWRX-300 and other #SMR technologies signals a transformative shift in how we approach clean and sustainable energy production. The partnerships between key players like TVA, Duke Energy, and American Electric Power reflect the industry’s confidence in the scalability, cost efficiency, and safety of SMRs. With their compact design and modular approach, SMRs like the BWRX-300 are poised to play a critical role in achieving global net-zero goals while ensuring reliable energy for future generations. Kudos to the teams driving this innovation forward💫
The US approach to SMRs is in complete contrast to the approach of the UK Government. The UK has seen endless rounds of "competition". Meanwhile other countries are moving straight to building SMRs.
Might Scott Strazik liaise with Jeff Lyash to look again at the figures on Page 3.18 of TVA’s 2025 Integrated Resource Plan (linked below) that puts the OCC for the FOAK BWRX-300 at $5.38 billion and the NOAK at $3.74 billion: https://tva-azr-eastus-cdn-ep-tvawcm-prd.azureedge.net/cdn-tvawcma/docs/default-source/environment/environmental-stewardship/integrated-resource-plan/2025/draft-2025-irp-volume-1-092324.pdf?sfvrsn=26f01b64_1 These figures are being bandied about by powerful social media figures like Chris Keefer MD to the great detriment of SMRs in general and the BWRX-300 in particular. Maybe Mr Lyash could ask his team to take into account this super-detailed, July 2024, INA Meta-Analysis of the costs of Advanced Nuclear Reactors: https://inldigitallibrary.inl.gov/sites/sti/sti/Sort_107010.pdf Page iv, Figure A-1: The 'median' OCC for the BOAK SMR ("... a reactor construction that occurs after the first demonstration...") puts the BWRX-300 at $2.4 billion, with an optimistic figure of $1.65 billion. Now, these figures appear commonsensical to anyone with a bit of engineering nous.
Isn't the story of nuclear life? Funding comes and goes but nothing happens. If modular why another 8 years? Still need to advance a "standard design"? What's been going on for the last several years? Always a bridesmaid never a bride. Don't hold your breath.
Supply chain improvements are critical to progressing safe, low-carbon nuclear energy. This is a significant development! #sustainableenergysolutons
Chugach Electric Association or GVEA should be investing in similar options for safe reliable baseload replacement and lead the state of Alaska in promoting a robust distributed energy power generating network.
We look forward to working with you and our other partners to advance nuclear technology in our region!
